did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning (specifically 19 state-of-the-art deep convolutional neural network architectures) to benchmark melanoma classification (binary classification of melanoma vs. non-melanoma) on the curated ISIC 2020 test set, achieving an AUC of 0.80.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work, including a duplicate removal strategy for ISIC datasets, the creation of a curated training set, and benchmarking using 19 deep learning architectures on the ISIC 2020 test set. While it includes a survey of prior literature (Table 1), its primary contributions are new data curation, analysis of dataset irregularities, and original experimental results (AUC of 0.80), classifying it as a research paper rather than a pure review.

title: Analysis of the ISIC image datasets: Usage, benchmarks and recommendations
brief_summary: This study analyzes the usage and integrity of the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) datasets from 2016 to 2020, identifying significant issues with duplicate images within and between training and testing sets. To address these irregularities, the authors propose a duplicate removal strategy and provide a curated, balanced training dataset for future research. They benchmarked 19 state-of-the-art deep convolutional neural network architectures on the curated ISIC 2020 test set for binary melanoma classification. The evaluation was conducted following the application of the duplicate removal strategy and data balancing steps. The best-performing model achieved an Area Under the Curve (AUC) of 0.80 for melanoma prediction. The paper concludes by highlighting dataset irregularities as potential research challenges and sharing the curated dataset resources on GitHub.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of melanoma vs. non-melanoma
all_task_types.details: The paper benchmarks 19 deep learning architectures on the curated ISIC 2020 test set for binary melanoma classification (melanoma vs. non-melanoma), achieving an AUC of 0.80. It also conducts further analysis on multi-class lesion diagnosis using the ISIC 2018 test set (7 classes: MEL, NV, BCC, AKIEC, BKL, DF, VASC) and binary classification on the ISIC 2017 test set (3 classes: MEL, NV, SK).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It reported descriptive statistics (AUC, accuracy, etc.) and cluster validity indices (Silhouette score, Calinski-Harabasz index, Davies-Bouldin index) to evaluate model performance and data separability, but did not perform inferential statistical hypothesis testing.

ISIC 2020 Test Set (Binary Melanoma Clas.metric: AUC (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristics Curve)
ISIC 2020 Test Set (Binary Melanoma Clas.notes: The best performing model was VGG19 with an AUC of 0.80. The mean AUC for all reported networks was 0.704 with a standard deviation of 0.071. Ground truth for ISIC 2020 test set was not publicly available; results are based on Kaggle leaderboard metrics.

ISIC 2017 Test Set (Binary Classificatio.notes: VGG19 demonstrated the highest accuracy (0.56). InceptionV3 had the lowest accuracy (0.30) but highest recall (0.94). EfficientNetB3 showed the highest precision (0.22). EfficientNetB4 showed the lowest recall (0.39).

ISIC 2018 Test Set (Multi-class Classifi.notes: Ground truth for ISIC 2018 test set was not publicly available; results evaluated on live leaderboard. Best baseline accuracy of 0.621 was achieved by EfficientNetB0 with a pretrained model. Other models achieved lower accuracies.

Curated Balanced Dataset (Separability A.metric: Intra-class distance (melanoma), Intra-class distance (non-melanoma), Inter-class distance, Calinski-Harabasz index (CH), Silhouette score (Si), Davies-Bouldin index (DB)
Curated Balanced Dataset (Separability A.notes: Analysis performed on the curated balanced dataset to measure separability of melanoma versus non-melanoma. EfficientNetB0 showed improved separability metrics compared to input.

architecture_used.main_model: 19 deep convolutional neural network architectures: DenseNet121, DenseNet169, DenseNet201, EfficientNetB0, EfficientNetB1, EfficientNetB2, EfficientNetB3, EfficientNetB4, InceptionResNetV2, InceptionV3, ResNet50, ResNet50V2, ResNet101, ResNet101V2, ResNet152, ResNet152V2, VGG16, VGG19, and Xception.
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning was not used for the primary benchmark experiments (baseline results). For multi-class classification on ISIC 2018, pretrained models based on ImageNet were used, with EfficientNetB0 achieving the best baseline accuracy.
architecture_used.input_size: Images were resized with the shortest side reduced to 224 pixels, followed by center-cropping to 224x224 pixels.

training_details.epochs: Trained for 50 epochs, with early stopping implemented until convergence (patience of 10 epochs). For multi-class analysis, max epochs set to 200 with early stopping based on validation accuracy.
training_details.hardware: Intel i7-7700 Quad Core 3.60GHz CPU, 64GB DDR4 2400MHz RAM, GTX1080 Ti 11GB GPU

classification_or_output_head.task: Binary classification (melanoma vs. non-melanoma) and Multi-class classification
classification_or_output_head.loss_function: Categorical crossentropy (used to address class imbalance in initial experiments)
classification_or_output_head.output_activation: Not explicitly stated, but implied binary/multi-class classification output

preprocessing_techniques: Duplicate removal strategy: Removal of 14,310 duplicate images from the training set (including binary identical duplicates and downsampled duplicates identified via filename suffix '_downsampled' and ISIC code matching).
Superpixel image removal: Removal of 2,000 superpixel images from ISIC 2017 training set and 600 from ISIC 2017 test set.
Data balancing: Creation of a curated balanced dataset with a 1:1 ratio of melanoma to non-melanoma classes (3,924 images each in training; 981 each in validation).
Image resizing: Images resized with the shortest side reduced to 224 pixels.
Center cropping: Images center-cropped to 224x224 pixels.
Data augmentation: Random rotations, random zooms, random width and height shifts, shearing, horizontal flipping, and vertical flipping.

conclusions: The ISIC datasets (2016-2020) contain a significant number of duplicate images both within individual datasets and across different datasets, including duplicates spread between training and testing sets.
Many prior studies utilizing ISIC datasets did not implement duplicate removal strategies, which may introduce bias into their results.
The authors propose a duplicate removal strategy and provide a curated, balanced training dataset to address these irregularities and mitigate bias.

Benchmarking 19 deep convolutional neural network architectures on the curated ISIC 2020 test set for binary melanoma classification yielded an Area Under the Curve (AUC) of 0.80 for the best-performing model (VGG19).
The study highlights that dataset irregularities, such as duplicates and class imbalance, present significant challenges for research in skin cancer detection.

The curated balanced dataset (1:1 ratio of melanoma to non-melanoma) is recommended for researchers to improve model generalization and address class imbalance issues inherent in the original ISIC datasets.
The authors acknowledge that while their duplicate removal strategy eliminates a large number of duplicates, edge cases (e.g., resized images without specific suffixes) may still exist.
The study provides baseline results without additional performance-maximizing strategies to establish a standard for future research using the curated datasets.
important_remarks: Significant data leakage risk: The study identified a large number of duplicate images within and between ISIC datasets (2016-2020), including duplicates spread across training and testing sets, which may introduce bias in prior research that did not implement duplicate removal.

Dataset bias in ISIC 2020: The ISIC 2020 test set contains only 690 unique patient IDs with a very low number of unique melanoma cases, potentially limiting the robustness of models trained exclusively on this dataset regarding generalization to different skin types.

Benchmark performance ceiling: The best-performing model (VGG19) achieved an AUC of 0.80 on the curated ISIC 2020 test set; the authors note this aim was not to maximize performance but to provide baselines, implying potential for higher accuracy with optimized strategies.

Sensitivity to image artifacts: Model performance was susceptible to noise from dermoscopic artifacts (e.g., hair, clinical markings, air pockets, ruler overlays), with some models showing improved accuracy when specific artifacts were removed, indicating potential for spurious feature learning.

Class imbalance issues: The original ISIC datasets exhibit severe class imbalance (e.g., ISIC 2020 training set had 27,124 'Other/Unknown' cases vs. 584 Melanoma cases), necessitating the authors' curated balanced dataset (1:1 ratio) to mitigate bias.

Lack of external validation: The study relies on internal ISIC datasets and Kaggle leaderboard metrics for the ISIC 2020 test set (ground truth not publicly available), limiting independent clinical validation.

Incomplete duplicate removal: The proposed duplicate removal strategy may miss edge cases, such as resized images without specific suffixes, meaning some duplicates may persist in the curated dataset.
